departments.concept: Organizational units of the clinic. Each department is a self-contained clinical division responsible for a specific area of patient care. This is the primary lens for financial and operational reporting — departmental Profit & Loss (P&L) is calculated as collected Revenue minus Staff Salaries and Supply Costs. Use this table when the question is about the performance, capacity, or cost-efficiency of a specific clinical area.

columns_with_descriptions.name: Clinical division name — the human-readable label for the department.
columns_with_descriptions.location: Physical location of the department within the hospital facility.
columns_with_descriptions.operating_budget: Annual financial budget allocated to this department. Represents the planned spending ceiling for the year.

staff.concept: Hospital human resources — covers all personnel from doctors and nurses to administrative staff. Staff salaries are the clinic's single largest fixed operating cost. The role field distinguishes clinical practitioners (doctors, nurses) from non-clinical staff. A staff member belongs to one department and may also serve as the head of that department — these are two separate relationships that should not be double-counted in financial analysis.

columns_with_descriptions.first_name: Given name of the staff member.
columns_with_descriptions.role: Professional designation of the staff member — distinguishes doctors from nurses and administrative personnel.
columns_with_descriptions.specialisation: Medical sub-specialty of the practitioner, applicable to doctors only (e.g., Cardiologist, Orthopedic Surgeon). Null for non-clinical staff.
columns_with_descriptions.department_id: The department this staff member is primarily assigned to.
columns_with_descriptions.salary: Annual gross salary of the staff member. The aggregate of salaries for a department represents its total personnel cost.

patients.concept: The central clinical registry. Every patient of the clinic has exactly one record here, capturing their demographics and ongoing risk status. Risk profile is the key clinical triage metric — it represents a patient's overall vulnerability and care priority. Date of birth is the source of truth for patient age calculations.

columns_with_descriptions.id: Unique clinical identifier for the patient.
columns_with_descriptions.date_of_birth: Patient's date of birth. Used to calculate current age.
columns_with_descriptions.nhi_number: National Health Index number — New Zealand's unique national patient identifier.
columns_with_descriptions.gender: Patient's gender identity. Relevant for demographic segmentation and gender-specific health screenings.
columns_with_descriptions.risk_profile: A calculated score between 0 and 100 representing the patient's overall clinical risk. Higher scores indicate greater vulnerability, urgency of care, and likelihood of adverse outcomes. Scores above 60 are typically considered high-risk.

appointments.concept: The central hub of clinical activity. Each appointment is a scheduled or completed encounter between one patient and one doctor in one department. Appointments are the bridge that connects patients to all downstream clinical data — lab results, billing, prescriptions, diagnoses, and clinical notes are all anchored to a specific appointment. Only 'Completed' appointments represent real clinical interactions; 'Cancelled' and 'No-show' appointments have no associated downstream records.

columns_with_descriptions.id: Unique identifier for the clinical encounter.
columns_with_descriptions.patient_id: The patient who attended this appointment.
columns_with_descriptions.doctor_id: The doctor who conducted this appointment. This is the authoritative record of which clinician treated which patient.
columns_with_descriptions.department_id: The clinical department in which this appointment took place.
columns_with_descriptions.appointment_date: The date and time of the clinical interaction.
columns_with_descriptions.status: The outcome of the appointment. 'Completed' means the visit occurred and downstream records exist. 'Cancelled' and 'No-show' mean the visit did not take place and no clinical or financial records were generated.
columns_with_descriptions.reason: The patient's stated reason for the visit at the time of booking.

billing.concept: The clinic's revenue record. Every completed appointment generates one billing invoice. 'Paid' invoices represent collected revenue; 'Pending' invoices represent money owed but not yet received. This table is the source of truth for all financial performance metrics, including departmental revenue and outstanding receivables.

columns_with_descriptions.appointment_id: The appointment this invoice was generated for. One appointment produces exactly one invoice.
columns_with_descriptions.amount: The monetary value charged for the service.
columns_with_descriptions.status: The payment state of the invoice — 'Paid' for collected revenue, 'Pending' for outstanding amounts.
columns_with_descriptions.payment_method: The method used to settle the invoice (e.g., Credit Card, Insurance, Cash).
columns_with_descriptions.invoice_date: The date the invoice was issued.

medical_supplies.concept: Inventory management for clinical consumables and equipment. Stock is managed at the department level — the same item can appear in multiple departments with different quantities. Facility-wide stock for any item is the sum across all departments. When quantity falls below the reorder level, that department needs restocking. Supply costs contribute to departmental operating expenses.

columns_with_descriptions.item_name: Name of the clinical product or consumable.
columns_with_descriptions.quantity: Current stock count for this item in this specific department.
columns_with_descriptions.reorder_level: The minimum stock threshold before this item needs to be restocked in this department.
columns_with_descriptions.supplier: The external vendor who provides this item.
columns_with_descriptions.department_id: The department that holds and manages this stock.
columns_with_descriptions.unit_cost: The cost per individual unit of this item.

lab_results.concept: Pathology and diagnostic test data. Each row represents a single test result from a specific visit. A patient can have multiple lab results from a single appointment. The is_abnormal flag is the primary clinical signal for identifying patients who need follow-up care. Lab results are always linked to an appointment, which in turn links to the patient and doctor.

columns_with_descriptions.appointment_id: The appointment during which this test was ordered and recorded.
columns_with_descriptions.test_name: The name of the diagnostic test performed.
columns_with_descriptions.result_value: The numerical or categorical value recorded for this test.
columns_with_descriptions.reference_range: The clinically normal range for this test. Results outside this range are flagged as abnormal.
columns_with_descriptions.is_abnormal: Indicates whether the test result falls outside the normal reference range. 'Yes' means the result is clinically significant and warrants review.

prescriptions.concept: Pharmacotherapy records. Each row is one medication prescribed during a specific visit. A single appointment can generate multiple prescriptions. Prescriptions are the record of the clinic's pharmacological treatment decisions and are useful for analysing medication patterns, polypharmacy risks, and treatment adherence.

columns_with_descriptions.appointment_id: The appointment during which this medication was prescribed.
columns_with_descriptions.medication: The name of the prescribed drug.
columns_with_descriptions.dosage: The prescribed strength and unit of the medication.
columns_with_descriptions.frequency: How often the medication should be taken.
columns_with_descriptions.duration: The total length of the prescribed treatment course.
columns_with_descriptions.notes: Any specific instructions or clinical warnings from the prescribing doctor.

clinical_notes.concept: Free-text narrative documentation written by the attending doctor for each completed appointment. These notes capture the clinician's observations, assessments, and plans in natural language. They are semantically indexed and searched via the RAG vector system — they are not suitable for structured SQL filtering. Use clinical_notes when the question requires interpreting narrative clinical reasoning or context.

columns_with_descriptions.appointment_id: The appointment this note belongs to. One appointment has at most one clinical note.
columns_with_descriptions.content: The full narrative text of the clinical note as written by the doctor.

diagnoses.concept: Structured clinical classifications recorded per encounter. Each appointment can yield multiple diagnoses — a primary condition (the main reason for the visit) and any secondary co-morbidities identified. This table is the source of truth for disease prevalence, diagnostic trends, and patient condition history. The is_primary flag distinguishes the principal complaint from incidental findings.

columns_with_descriptions.appointment_id: The appointment during which this diagnosis was recorded.
columns_with_descriptions.condition_name: The clinical or colloquial name of the diagnosed condition.
columns_with_descriptions.icd_code: The standardised ICD-10 classification code for this condition — used for precise medical coding and insurance reporting.
columns_with_descriptions.is_primary: Whether this was the main reason for the visit ('Yes') or a secondary/incidental finding ('No').

clinical_guidelines.concept: The clinic's institutional knowledge base — containing treatment protocols, clinical best-practice guidelines, and hospital policy documents. These records are not tied to any patient or encounter. They are exclusively searched via the RAG semantic vector system and should never be queried with SQL for patient-level analysis.

columns_with_descriptions.title: The name of the protocol or policy document.
columns_with_descriptions.category: The clinical area or department this guideline applies to.
columns_with_descriptions.content: The full text of the guideline, semantically indexed for RAG retrieval.
